title: Locality versus Fock-space structure in East-type models
abstract: Local kinetic constraints in quantum many-body systems can generate slow dynamics or complete many-body localisation. Here we focus on a modification of the quantum East model: Inspired by random matrix theory, we randomise the connectivity in Fock space (rendering it nonlocal in real space) while preserving its organisation into neighbouring magnetisation sectors. We find that there is still a transition between two distinct phases, one delocalised and the other localised. We conclude that, for East-type constrained models, the essential ingredient is the structure of the graph in Fock space rather than geometric locality of spin flips.
summary: The paper demonstrates that the localization transition in East-type models is determined by the connectivity structure in Fock space rather than real-space locality. By scrambling the real-space connections while keeping the magnetization-sector structure intact, the authors show that the phase transition remains qualitatively unchanged. This implies that the hierarchical organization of the Fock-space graph is the primary driver of localization in these constrained systems.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether the localization transition in East-type models is driven by real-space geometric locality or by the underlying connectivity structure in Fock space.
main_result: The study finds that the localization-delocalization transition persists even when real-space locality is removed via random permutations, suggesting that the hierarchical structure of the Fock-space graph is the essential ingredient for localization.
method: The authors use a graph-theoretic framework to analyze the Hamiltonian and perform numerical simulations including finite-size scaling analysis of Shannon entropy and dynamical participation ratios.
model_or_system: The study focuses on the Quantum East Model and a newly introduced 'Permuted East Model', which preserves magnetization-sector organization but randomizes connectivity in Fock space to remove real-space locality.
key_observables: Dynamical participation ratio, Eigenstate Shannon entropy, Shannon-entropy fluctuations, and Local spectral variance.
important_parameters: The hopping rate parameter (s) which controls the transition, system size (L), and the disorder parameter (w).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the connectivity in Fock space can be effectively modeled by random permutations while preserving the magnetization sector structure.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the construction of the permuted model; Figure 2 illustrates the Hamiltonian matrix patterns and adjacency graphs; Figure 3 shows the dynamical participation ratio scaling; Figure 4 shows Shannon entropy fluctuations; Figure 5 shows mean scaled Shannon entropy; Figure 6 shows local spectral variance and extrapolated transition points.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of localization in constrained models, defines the permuted East model, presents numerical results comparing the standard and permuted models using various diagnostics, and concludes with an extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it identifies that the topology of the Hilbert space graph, rather than physical distance, can be the fundamental driver of many-body localization and slow dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic of the permuted East construction. Each matrix represents the Hamiltonian in the Fock-state basis, with rows and columns ordered so that states with the same number of up spins M form contiguous blocks (shaded grey on the diagonal). The diagonal entries (blue dots on the main diagonal) represent the on-site disorder, which is identical in both models. Only nearest-neighbour blocks (M ↔M ± 1, light blue) carry off-diagonal entries, reflecting the single- spin-flip structure. In the East model (left) the nonzero off-diagonal entries within each block follow the structured, constraint-driven pattern imposed by real-space locality. In the permuted East model (right) the...

Figure 2. Panels (a-c) show the locations of the nonzero matrix elements in the Hamiltonians of the standard Ising, quantum East and permuted East models. Panels (d-f) show the graphs described by the corresponding adjacency matrices, obtained from the Hamiltonians in panels (a-c) via Eq. (2). The horizontal and vertical axes in panels (a-c) correspond to Fock state labels, ordered so that Fock states with the same number of up spins M = P

Figure 3. Infinite-time average of the dynamical participation ratio ϕ∞= limt→∞ϕ(t), defined in Eq. (5), for all three models with w = 0 (no Fock-space disorder) and s ranging from −0.4 to 0.4 in steps of 0.1. Initial states are the L single-spin-up product states; ϕ∞is averaged over these initial states and, for the permuted East model, also over realisations of the random permutation. Because these are low-entanglement states in the M = 1 magnetisation sector, they probe the low-energy part of the spectrum rather than the bulk, so the results are not representative of the full spectral average. In the fully delocalised limit ϕ∞= O(D−1), while in the fully localised limit ϕ∞= O(1). Each...

Figure 4. Representative examples of the Shannon entropy for each eigenstate of the East model for system size L = 10 and w = 1. Plots for the permuted model are similar, and data for both models is analysed in Fig. 6. (a) Entropy of each eigenstate versus its energy, for two values of s each represented by a different colour. The lines in a lighter shade of each colour show the local averages over a small window in energy. Note how the fluctuations of the larger s data around the mean are larger, as expected for a system violating ETH (see text). (b) Same data but with the local mean over the window ∆E subtracted to demonstrate fluctuations around the microcanonical average. A systematic...

Figure 5. Mean scaled Shannon entropy µS/ ln D for the East (panel (a)) and permuted East (b) models, averaged over all eigenstates and disorder realisations (number of realisations indicated in the legend) for each size L. Here w = 1. As s increases the mean decreases, consistent with increasing loc- alisation. For a quantitative determination of the transition point see Fig. 6.
